presentation_variants.presenting_complaint: I lost consciousness and my family says I had a seizure
I blacked out and woke up confused with sore muscles
My family witnessed me shaking all over
I don't remember the event — I just woke up confused
I had a sudden loss of consciousness with convulsions

history_data.hpi: Patient was witnessed by family members to suddenly lose consciousness without warning while sitting at home. Approximately 30 seconds after losing consciousness, patient developed generalized rhythmic shaking movements of all four limbs lasting approximately 90 seconds. Following the event, patient remained confused and disoriented for 15 minutes before gradually returning to baseline. Patient reports not remembering the event itself. Bystanders noted tongue biting and possible incontinence. No prior seizures. This is patient's first seizure event.
patient_quotes: The next thing I knew, people were standing around me.
I felt very confused and tired afterward.
I apparently bit my tongue — I can feel it now.
pmh_pool: Recent head injury — minor head bump 2 days ago
Sleep deprivation — only 4 hours sleep last night

social_history.stress: Recent work stress and sleep deprivation
social_history.sleep: Only 4 hours sleep last night — known trigger

negative_history: No focal weakness or numbness before event
No recent head trauma except minor bump 2 days ago
No fever at time of seizure (resolved yesterday)

general_appearance: Mild discomfort — tongue bite visible inside mouth
neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, time after 15 minutes
Tongue bite evident on intraoral exam

investigations.Blood Glucose: 4.8 mmol/L — normal. Hypoglycaemia excluded as precipitant.
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes: Na 138 mmol/L, K 3.9 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, Urea 6.1 mmol/L — all normal. Hyponatraemia and hypokalaemia excluded as causes of seizure.
investigations.FBC (Full Blood Count): WBC 9.2 × 10⁹/L, Hb 13.8 g/dL, Platelets 268 × 10⁹/L — normal. No evidence of acute infection or anaemia.
investigations.Lactate: 3.2 mmol/L (mildly elevated) — consistent with post-ictal metabolic state from seizure activity and muscle exertion. Expected to normalize.
investigations.Urea & Electrolytes (repeat if needed): To monitor for any electrolyte disturbance; initial normal.
investigations.CT Head: No acute intracranial abnormality. No mass, bleed, or structural lesion. Normal ventricles. No midline shift. Minor head injury 2 days ago did not result in any pathology.

essential_questions: loss of consciousness — was it sudden?
were there any witnesses to the event?
describe what happened during the event
how long did the shaking last?
any tongue biting or cheek injury?
how long was confusion after the event?
any warning or aura before loss of consciousness?
previous seizures or family history of seizures?
essential_examinations: assessment for tongue bite or incontinence
critical_actions: recognize seizure from eyewitness account and postictal state
check blood glucose immediately — rule out hypoglycemia
establish IV access and cardiac monitoring
obtain CT head to rule out structural cause
check electrolytes and basic metabolic panel
obtain 12-lead ECG to rule out cardiac syncope
counsel on seizure precautions and driving restrictions
red_flags: First seizure — requires investigation for cause
Prolonged postictal confusion (>30 min indicates potential status epilepticus)
Recurrent seizures in close succession — status epilepticus risk
management_actions: Immediate: Ensure airway patency, position on side, clear environment of hazards
Blood glucose check — if <3.5 mmol/L, give IV dextrose 50mL of 50% solution
12-lead ECG to rule out cardiac syncope
Blood tests: FBC, Urea & Electrolytes, Lactate, Blood Glucose
CT head without contrast to rule out structural lesion
Neurology urgent referral — first seizure workup
Do NOT start anticonvulsants after single first unprovoked seizure without neurology input (risk of recurrence ~40%)
Counsel on seizure precautions: driving restrictions (1–6 months depending on jurisdiction), avoid heights, water safety
EEG when safe (may show post-ictal changes or epileptiform activity — arrange with neurology)
Educate on seizure triggers: sleep deprivation, stress, fever, alcohol, flickering lights
critical_history_questions: Was the event witnessed? What exactly did they see?
Did you lose consciousness suddenly without warning?
How long did the shaking last?
Did you wet yourself or lose bowel control?
How long were you confused afterward?
Any warning signs or aura before it happened?
Have you ever had a seizure before?
Does anyone in your family have epilepsy or seizures?
Have you had a head injury recently?
Have you had any recent fever or infection?
How much sleep have you had recently?
Do you drink alcohol? Any recent binge drinking?
Any recent use of drugs or alcohol?
